name: 01_helpful_vs_unhelpful
description: Detailed factual answer vs. vague non-answer. Tests relevance + completeness + specificity.
prompt: How does the global interpreter lock affect Python multi-threaded performance for CPU-bound and I/O-bound work?
response_a: The global interpreter lock in CPython forces Python multi-threaded code to serialize: only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time. For CPU-bound work, multi-threaded performance therefore matches single-threaded performance — threads do not run in parallel. For I/O-bound work, the interpreter lock is released during the blocking call (network, disk, subprocess), so threads overlap their wait time and you do see real concurrency. To unlock CPU-bound parallelism, use multiprocessing (separate processes, separate locks), C extensions that release the lock such as NumPy or Cython nogil, or the Python 3.13 free-threaded build (PEP 703).
response_b: The interpreter lock is a thing in Python that affects code. It can sometimes slow things down. Multi-threaded code is complicated. Performance varies, and you might want to look into other approaches.
expected_rationale: Response A repeats every prompt anchor (global interpreter lock, multi-threaded, performance, CPU-bound, I/O-bound) and introduces concrete entities (PEP 703, multiprocessing, NumPy, Cython) for specificity. Response B drops the CPU/I/O distinction entirely, tanking completeness.
